briefing.incidentSummary:
A USB drive was found in a company parking lot near the employee entrance.

The device was passed from security to management to IT before forensic analysis could begin.

When IT finally examined it, a suspicious file — unknown.exe — had appeared on the drive. It wasn't there originally.

Somebody introduced it during the chain of custody. But who?
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Reconstruct who handled the USB and when

› Identify the window in which unknown.exe appeared

› Spot the missing documentation steps that broke the chain

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Security Guard

suspicionTrack.reason: Had initial physical access to the USB
No system interaction detected during their possession

digitalFootprint.deviceUsage: Security Desk terminal — no USB connection detected

suspects.profileNotes: Found the USB in the parking lot at 09:50. Kept it at the security desk briefly before handing it to the department manager. No workstation or computer access occurred during this window. Physical handling only — no digital interaction.

suspects.name: Department Manager

suspicionTrack.reason: USB was actively connected to a workstation during their possession
unknown.exe appears at 10:17 — squarely within the manager's custody window (10:05–10:25)

digitalFootprint.deviceUsage: Manager Workstation — USB device connection logged at 10:06

suspects.profileNotes: Received the USB from security at 10:05 and connected it to their personal workstation to 'inspect the contents' before forwarding to IT. This is a direct violation of evidence handling protocol. The suspicious file unknown.exe was created at 10:17 — 11 minutes after the USB was connected to this workstation. Whether the file was intentionally placed or copied via malware/auto-run is not confirmed, but this manager is the only one who connected the device to a computer before IT received it.

suspects.name: IT Technician

suspicionTrack.reason: Technical capability to introduce files onto a USB
Received device at 10:25 — after unknown.exe already existed

suspicionTrack.label: Moderate — received after file appeared, unlikely to be the source

digitalFootprint.deviceUsage: Forensic Workstation — USB connected at 10:26 for imaging

suspects.profileNotes: Received the USB from the manager at 10:25. By this point, unknown.exe already existed on the drive (created at 10:17). Began forensic imaging preparation according to protocol. No evidence linking them to the file's creation. High technical skill but received the contaminated device — not the source of contamination.

suspects.name: Unknown External Actor

suspicionTrack.reason: USB found in parking lot — could have been planted or infected externally
Auto-run malware or OS-triggered file creation cannot be ruled out

suspicionTrack.label: Moderate — contamination may have been automatic, not deliberate

digitalFootprint.deviceUsage: Unknown — possible prior infection of the USB
digitalFootprint.locationTrace: Unknown — device found in parking lot

suspects.profileNotes: The USB was found in a public area before any chain of custody was established. It is possible the drive was pre-infected or that unknown.exe was introduced automatically when the manager connected it to their workstation via auto-run or background OS processes. Without proper forensic isolation at the point of discovery, this cannot be conclusively ruled out. However, the manager's decision to connect the device is the identifiable failure point.

items.label: USB Drive File List
items.detail: The USB drive contains three files. salary.xlsx and bonus_data.xlsx are legitimate HR documents — both created at 10:02, consistent with their origin from the HR department's internal systems. A third file, unknown.exe, is an executable program. It was not part of the original HR document set. It was created at 10:17 — 15 minutes after the HR files, and after the USB had been found and passed through the chain of custody. The file has no business justification for being on the drive.

items.label: Chain of Custody Log — Missing Documentation
items.detail: The custody log reveals multiple critical failures. The USB device was never placed in an evidence bag. No custody tag was applied. No official transfer form was completed between the security guard and the manager, or between the manager and IT. The device was not photographed or documented at the point of discovery. Additionally, the device was connected to at least one workstation before forensic acquisition. Each of these failures violates standard digital forensic evidence handling protocols. Because the chain is broken, it is legally impossible to definitively prove that unknown.exe was not on the drive when it was found — or that it was not introduced at multiple points.

items.label: USB Connection — Manager Workstation
items.detail: Workstation logs confirm that a USB mass storage device was connected to the Department Manager's workstation at 10:06. The device remained connected until 10:22. During this window, at 10:17, unknown.exe was created on the drive. The file creation timestamp places it squarely within the manager's custody and within the active USB connection session. No other workstation connection is recorded for this device prior to the IT Technician's forensic workstation at 10:26.

minigame.title: File Appearance Mapper
minigame.description: Match the unknown.exe creation time (10:17) to the correct handler window on the timeline bar. Which handler had the device when the file appeared?
minigame.hint: Place 10:17 on the timeline and see which handler's window it falls within.
hints: 10:17 is after 10:05 and before 10:25 — that's the manager's window.
The Security Guard had the device from 09:50–10:05. IT had it from 10:25 onward. 10:17 falls in between — with the manager.

onSuccess.message: FILE ORIGIN CONFIRMED — unknown.exe was created at 10:17, during the Department Manager's custody window. The manager connected the USB to their workstation at 10:06. The file appeared 11 minutes later.

timeline.title: USB Found in Parking Lot
timeline.description: Security guard discovers USB device near the employee entrance. No evidence bag or documentation.

timeline.title: HR Files on Drive
timeline.description: salary.xlsx and bonus_data.xlsx created at 10:02 — the only legitimate files on the device.

timeline.title: USB Handed to Manager
timeline.description: Security guard passes device to Department Manager. No transfer form. No custody tag.

timeline.title: Manager Connects USB to Workstation
timeline.description: Manager plugs device into personal workstation to inspect contents — direct protocol violation.

timeline.title: unknown.exe Appears
timeline.description: Suspicious executable created on the USB drive during the active connection to the manager's workstation.

timeline.title: USB Passed to IT
timeline.description: Manager forwards device to IT Technician. unknown.exe already exists at this point.

timeline.title: IT Forensic Imaging Begins
timeline.description: IT Technician connects device to forensic workstation. Discovers unknown.exe. Investigation launched.

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Department Manager identified — connected the USB before forensic isolation, introducing the suspicious file.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but the chain of custody breakdown needs more documentation.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The evidence points to the Department Manager — the only one who connected the device to a workstation before IT.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

coldCase.title: Case Gone Cold
coldCase.subtitle: No evidence collected. The origin of unknown.exe remains unexplained.
coldCase.label: COLD CASE

aria.welcomeToHub:
...A USB in a parking lot. Passed hand to hand.
By the time the right people touched it — it wasn't clean anymore.
Find the moment it changed.
aria.exploreFeeds:
File timestamps. Custody logs. Workstation connections.
One person connected this drive before they should have.
aria.viewEvidence:
The file appeared at 10:17. Three people had the device.
Only one connected it to a computer.
aria.decryptionHint: Match the file creation time to the handler windows. One window fits perfectly.
aria.flagSuspect:
You're close.
The broken chain tells the story — find who broke it and where.
